question: Micron reported fiscal second-quarter 2026 revenue of $23.86 billion, far above the $8.05 billion reported a year earlier, as AI-driven memory demand accelerated.
Salesforce delivered record fourth-quarter fiscal 2026 revenue of $11.2 billion and remaining performance obligation of $72.4 billion, reflecting continued enterprise demand.
Snowflake reported fourth-quarter fiscal 2026 product revenue of $1.23 billion, up 30% year over year, with remaining performance obligations rising 42%.
Seagate forecast fourth-quarter revenue of about $3.45 billion, above analyst expectations, as AI adoption boosted demand for data-storage hardware.
ServiceNow reported 22% revenue growth in the first quarter of 2026, but its shares fell after investors focused on lower margin guidance and delayed large deals.
Robinhood missed first-quarter 2026 revenue and profit expectations as cryptocurrency revenue fell 47% from the prior year.
Eli Lilly reported fourth-quarter 2025 revenue growth of 43% to $19.3 billion, driven by strong volume growth from Mounjaro and Zepbound.
Novo Nordisk shares declined after Canada approved a generic version of Ozempic, increasing concerns about future pricing pressure for GLP-1 drugs.
Target reported that full-year 2025 net sales declined 1.7% and adjusted earnings per share fell from the prior year.
Lowe's fourth-quarter 2025 comparable sales increased 1.3%, but its 2026 outlook pointed to only flat-to-2% comparable-sales growth.
$MU just printed record revenue as AI memory demand keeps running way beyond available supply.
$CRM sitting on more than $72B in RPO while Q4 revenue grows double digits makes the enterprise AI story look solid.
$SNOW product revenue up 30% and RPO up 42% makes the data-cloud growth story look very much alive.
$STX guiding above expectations because AI storage demand is so strong is exactly the kind of update storage bulls wanted.
$NOW revenue growth was fine, but lower margin guidance and delayed deals make the stock reaction hard to ignore.
$HOOD crypto revenue falling 47% is rough, especially when the quarter missed on both revenue and profit.
$LLY Mounjaro and Zepbound are carrying growth again, with Q4 sales jumping more than 40%.
$NVO getting hit by Canada approving generic Ozempic makes the pricing-risk story feel real.
$TGT sales down and EPS lower for the year is not a great setup for a retail turnaround.
$LOW comps finally turned positive, but the housing backdrop still makes the recovery feel cautious.
We ended fiscal 2026 with annual recurring revenue of $5.25 billion, up 24% year over year, and fourth-quarter revenue increased 23%.
Adjusted EBITDA reached $780 million in the fourth quarter, up 38% from the prior year, as marketplace scale improved.
Net revenues increased 4% to $8.7 billion, and transaction margin dollars rose 3% during the quarter.
Organic sales increased 3% across our portfolio, although higher commodity costs are expected to pressure earnings.
We raised our 2026 operating profit outlook after first-quarter profit exceeded expectations.
Comparable restaurant sales rose slightly, but operating margin declined due to higher beef and labor costs.
We maintained our full-year profit outlook after the quarterly beat, reflecting continued caution around medical cost trends.
We raised our full-year adjusted profit forecast after improved cost controls lowered the medical loss ratio.
Same-store sales improved meaningfully, but higher staffing investments kept operating margins below prior-year levels.
Fourth-quarter net sales and comparable sales both declined, indicating that store traffic and discretionary demand remain weak.
Investors urged Alphabet to strengthen safeguards around the use of its cloud and AI technologies in government surveillance and military contexts.
Organizations asked U.S. trade officials to use new import bans and duties against goods linked to forced labor risks in global supply chains.
Health and environmental groups sued the EPA over the rollback of mercury standards for coal-fired power plants, warning of higher pollution risks.
Several U.S. states challenged the EPA's decision to roll back the legal basis for vehicle greenhouse-gas rules, creating uncertainty around climate regulation.
Reports showed that diversity, equity, and inclusion policies were falling down corporate priority lists amid political pressure.
Proxy adviser ISS urged ConocoPhillips shareholders to support an independent board chair proposal, citing the need for stronger management oversight.
Companies joining a new renewables initiative pledged 0.1% of revenue to help fund global clean-power expansion.
Corporate power-purchase agreements continued to support new wind and solar capacity as companies sought cleaner electricity for AI-era operations.
AI tools are improving access to sustainability data, but experts warned that ESG reporting still requires human oversight and strong controls.
Analysts raised longer-term EU carbon-price forecasts as falling free allowances were expected to increase incentives for emissions reduction.
The U.K. economy grew 0.5% in the three months to February 2026, improving from weak growth at the end of 2025.
France's GDP grew only 0.9% in 2025, slowing from 1.1% growth in 2024 and 1.6% growth in 2023.
Spain's economy expanded 2.8% in 2025, far outgrowing many European peers.
Saudi Arabia's real GDP grew 4.5% in 2025, supported by stronger oil activity and continued non-oil growth.
The United Arab Emirates announced it would leave OPEC, weakening the oil cartel and adding uncertainty to global energy markets.
Spain's unemployment rate rose to 10.83% in the first quarter of 2026, although it remained the lowest first-quarter level since 2008.
Vietnam's first-quarter GDP growth slowed to 7.83% from 8.46% in the prior quarter as higher input and energy costs created policy challenges.
Malaysia's economy grew 5.3% year over year in the first quarter of 2026, showing continued expansion despite some moderation from late 2025.
Fitch revised the Philippines' outlook to negative as high energy prices and weak public capital spending were expected to weigh on growth.
Taiwan's export orders surged at the fastest pace in more than 16 years as AI and high-performance computing demand expanded.
